Creator of the Garden

Sergey Dronov

Artist · Jeweller · Garden Creator

Sergey Dronov is a Russian artist whose creative work brings together decorative arts, jewellery, landscape composition, and a profound respect for the natural world.

He graduated from the Jewellery Department of the Moscow School of Artistic Crafts, where he mastered the traditional techniques of handcrafted jewellery, composition, and ornamental design. He later studied gemmology at the Moscow Geological Prospecting Institute, developing a scientific understanding of minerals and natural materials that continues to influence his artistic vision.

Since 2002
Member of the Union of Artists of Russia. For many years he has participated in international exhibitions, presenting collections of jewellery and small-scale decorative art.
Jury & Editorial
He has served as a jury member for international jewellery exhibitions, and as a member of the editorial board of the international women's magazine Sunmag, where he advises authors and editors on landscape design, gardens, and the artistic relationship between architecture and nature.
Collections
His jewellery and works of decorative art are held in private collections in the United Arab Emirates, Italy, the United States, France, and Argentina.
Jewellery

A Parallel Craft

Alongside the garden, jewellery remains Sergey Dronov's original discipline — the same eye for proportion, material and colour, worked in gold, gemstone and pearl rather than tree and stone.

"One hand, two disciplines — gold and gemstone under the loupe, fern and stone under the open sky."

Practice

A Continuation of the Same Artistic Discipline

For Sergey Dronov, creating a garden is a continuation of his artistic practice. Just as a jeweller carefully balances proportion, texture, colour and light within a single piece, he composes landscapes using trees, mosses, ferns, shrubs, water and stone.

Every path, every view, and every carefully preserved century-old tree reflects the same artistic philosophy: nature should not be dominated but understood, protected, and allowed to reveal its own beauty.

A Jeweller's Structure

The Garden Gate

The hand-built wooden gate marks not so much an entrance to the garden as a passage into another space — from the everyday into the stillness of nature. Two lanterns, lit at dusk, become symbolic beacons, inviting one to leave the outside world at the threshold.

Crossing through this gate, a person finds themselves among century-old spruces, moss and ferns, where time moves more slowly and attention returns to the beauty of every detail. What begins here is not a walk, but an inward journey — into a world of light, nature and contemplation.

Season by Season

A Structure That Ages With the Garden

Every carefully preserved century-old tree, and every crafted structure placed among them, is composed to be seen from multiple angles and across every season — the same principle of harmony he applies to a finished piece of jewellery.

A Sound Sculpture

Bells at the Threshold

The brass bells form a kind of sound sculpture with no fixed shape of its own. It is shaped by the wind, the movement of the gate, and the turning of the seasons. In summer, their ringing dissolves into the rustle of ferns and leaves; in winter, it becomes especially clear in the silence of the snow-covered forest.

This almost imperceptible sound marks the beginning of an inward journey — a passage into a space where nature is perceived as art, and art becomes a way of experiencing nature more deeply.

Water Bowls for Birds and Forest Animals

A Source of Life

Massive bowls, hand-shaped from architectural concrete, have become part of the garden's artistic concept — one in which art does not stand apart from nature, but serves it. Over time their surfaces have become covered with moss and lichen, so that the man-made form has gradually come to feel like a natural part of the forest landscape.

Filled with rainwater, these stone basins become, every day, a place where birds, squirrels and other forest creatures come to drink. Here the art object finds its true purpose — not only as an element of contemporary landscape composition, but as a source of life. This is the philosophy of the garden: true beauty is born when what is made by human hands begins to serve nature.

Landscape Architecture

The Architecture of a Natural Flow

The stream runs through the whole garden along a natural course, shaped over many years by melting snow and rain. Rather than redirecting its flow, the banks were built from boulders and river pebbles so as to seem as though they had always been there, only underlining the water's own geometry.

Water, stone and the reflection of the sky form a living composition that constantly changes with the season and the light. The stream becomes not only the garden's compositional axis, but a symbol of life's continuous movement — here, landscape architecture does not compete with nature, but helps reveal its own perfection.

A Living Work

Ferns and Moss

The garden "Ferns and Moss" is entirely created and maintained by the artist himself. It is a living work of art, shaped not only by design but also by time, changing seasons, and continuous care.

At the heart of Sergey Dronov's work lies a simple belief: true beauty is born from craftsmanship, patience, respect for nature, and respect for every living thing.

Above All

More than a garden, Ferns and Moss is a space of creative inspiration — cultivated, season after season, to give Professor Nona Dronova, artist and scientist, the conditions in which to paint.

Every tree preserved, every path composed, every hour of quiet care is offered in service of that work — a garden made not only to be seen, but to make art possible.

See the paintings this garden has made possible.
